summary: This notebook serves as a comprehensive exploratory data analysis (EDA) for the NFL Big Data Bowl 2025 dataset, consolidating insights from a series of prior analyses. It demonstrates how to load, merge, and clean four core datasets (games, plays, player_play, players) while systematically handling missing values and structural conflicts. The author performs extensive statistical aggregation and visualization to uncover team-level offensive and defensive performance trends, player contributions, and temporal patterns across the season. Readers should take away a clear understanding of the dataset's structure, key feature distributions, and how to derive meaningful tactical insights from raw play-by-play and tracking data without relying on machine learning models.
competition_flow: Loads and merges four NFL CSV datasets, cleans missing values, computes aggregated offensive/defensive statistics, and generates comprehensive team and temporal visualizations to identify performance trends.
data_reading: pd.read_csv loads games.csv, player_play.csv, players.csv, and plays.csv from the Kaggle input directory.
data_processing: Merges DataFrames on gameId, playId, and nflId using inner joins; renames conflicting penaltyYards columns; imputes missing values by filling object columns with mode, numeric columns with mean, and boolean columns with mode; filters to selected columns for analysis.
features_engineering: Derives composite defensive metrics (totalTackles, totalFumbles, totalSackYards, totalTacklesForLoss) by summing related columns; aggregates yards and action counts by team, player (nflId), and game date; computes pairwise correlations and group-wise descriptive statistics.
insights: Passing and receiving plays collectively dominate total yardage, while rushing plays contribute less but remain strategically vital.
Teams like JAX, GB, KC, and SF excel at gaining yards after the catch, whereas HOU and TB struggle with ball security.
Defensive pressure and sack metrics vary significantly by team, with PIT and BUF showing strong pass-rush capabilities.
Correlations between major offensive yardage metrics are near zero, indicating they operate largely independently at the play level.
Missing data is heavily concentrated in defensive flags and penalty names, requiring careful imputation or exclusion for downstream modeling.
critical_findings: Receiving yards total slightly exceeds passing yards total, highlighting the value of yards after catch in the dataset.
Pairwise correlations between rushing, passing, and receiving yards are extremely weak (~-0.007 to 0.003), suggesting play-type yardage is not linearly coupled.
Defensive metrics like causedPressure and wasInitialPassRusher have substantial missingness, indicating they are only recorded for specific play types or players.
